title: Scandinavian rosettes
subtitle: Deep-fried iron-moulded pastry dusted with icing sugar
excerpt: Scandinavian rosettes are thin, crisp fried pastries made by dipping a preheated cast-iron mould into a light batter and then straight into hot oil. The batter sets and crisps around the hot iron, forming a delicate lacy shell that slides free once fried. The iron must be very hot before dipping into the batter.
sourceNotes: Rosette pastries are a Scandinavian and Central European tradition, particularly associated with Swedish and Norwegian Christmas baking. The technique of dipping a preheated metal mould into batter and frying it is also found in South Asian thokulumbe and Mexican buñuelos de viento, reflecting a shared culinary history of fried iron-moulded pastry. This recipe is synthesised from the Scandinavian rosette tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rosettes are best made and eaten on the same day. The batter can be mixed an hour ahead.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: deep-fried at 175 degrees C, not oven-baked

glossaryTerms.definition: The rosette iron must be preheated in the oil for at least 2 minutes before the first dip. A cold iron will stick to the batter rather than releasing cleanly. After each rosette, return the iron to the oil for 30 seconds to reheat before dipping again. Dip only to about three-quarters of the iron's depth: if the batter goes over the top, the rosette will not release. When the rosette is done, it slides or drops free from the iron; if it does not, gently ease it off with a fork.

content.text: The technique here is all in

correctly. The batter itself is simple and takes five minutes to mix. Make the first rosette as a test: adjust the batter thickness with a splash more milk if it is too thick, a tablespoon more flour if too thin. The batter should coat the iron in a layer about as thick as a credit card.
Deep-frying: keep the oil at 175 degrees C throughout. Use a probe thermometer and adjust the heat between batches. Never leave hot oil unattended.
Whisk the 2 eggs, 240 ml milk, 120 g flour, 15 g sugar and quarter teaspoon salt in a bowl until smooth. Leave to rest for 10 minutes.

Heat the oil to 175 degrees C in a wide, deep pan. Submerge the rosette iron in the hot oil and heat for 2 minutes. Lift the iron, shake off the excess oil, and dip it immediately into the batter to about three-quarters of its depth. Return the iron straight to the hot oil. Fry for 1 to 2 minutes until the rosette is deep golden and pulls away from the iron. Remove with tongs, shake off the oil and place on a cooling rack. Return the iron to the oil for 30 seconds before the next rosette. Dust with icing sugar while still warm.

items.problem: Batter sticks to the iron and does not release
items.solution: The iron was not hot enough when dipped. Return it to the oil for at least a minute and test with a drop of batter: it should sizzle and set instantly. Also check the iron is fully submerged in oil during preheating.

items.problem: Rosettes are thick and doughy rather than crisp and lacy
items.solution: The batter is too thick. Add milk a tablespoon at a time until the batter coats the iron in a thin, even layer. Also ensure the oil is at 175 degrees C before frying.
